design_philosophy.core_loop: Choose a character class, navigate interconnected hub levels solving puzzles and combat encounters, acquire items and weapons, progress to next hub area, confront Korax
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Hub-based exploration vs. combat readiness: players must backtrack between areas to solve multi-stage puzzles while managing limited resources across three distinct playstyles
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embody one of three archetypes (Fighter/Cleric/Mage) with fundamentally different approaches to overcome a dark realm controlled by a Serpent Rider
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hexen is opinionated about non-linear level design within structured progression. It rejects pure linearity (unlike Doom II) by introducing hub-based architecture where players traverse between central areas and side levels, solving large-scale environmental puzzles requiring item collection and switch activation across multiple visits. Three distinct character classes force replayability and make playstyle choice consequential—items behave differently per class (e.g., flechette functions differently for Fighter vs. Mage), ensuring class selection meaningfully impacts moment-to-moment gameplay rather than serving as cosmetic variation.

difficulty.checkpointing: Hub-based checkpoint system: player progress saved upon entering hub areas; death resets player to current hub with enemies respawned
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High: three character classes with dramatically different attributes (Fighter: toughest/fastest; Mage: most fragile/slowest with long-range spells; Cleric: balanced melee/ranged) allow players to select difficulty implicitly. Character choice determines combat pacing and resource management strategy.

progression.upgrade_system: Class-specific weapon progression (Fighter: mundane and magical melee; Cleric: melee/ranged hybrid; Mage: spell arsenal) plus shared inventory items (Disc of Repulsion, Icon of the Defender, flechette) with class-dependent functionality
progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential hub unlocking through multi-stage environmental puzzle solving: items and switches acquired in side levels enable progression to next hub. Players must revisit hubs with collected items to activate blocked passages
replayability_hooks: Three distinct character classes with unique weapons, speed, toughness, and item interactions force replay with different playstyles
Non-linear hub design encourages exploration and discovery of alternative routes and puzzle solutions
Class-specific item behavior variation (e.g., flechette functions differ per class) rewards experimenting with different archetypes

world_building_techniques: Sequential realm progression: elemental dungeons → wilderness → seminary → castle → necropolis creates a thematic journey
Serpent Riders trilogy narrative continuity: Hexen follows Heretic's D'Sparil defeat and precedes Hexen II, situating player in established lore
Character class selection as narrative choice: Fighter/Cleric/Mage archetypes hint at different motivations/backstories for confronting Korax

boss_design.philosophy: Korax serves as a capstone confrontation representing the second Serpent Rider; positioned at end of extended journey through elemental realms and stronghold, functioning as climactic narrative payoff rather than stage-gating mechanic
notable_bosses: Korax (final boss, Serpent Rider): encountered after traversing necropolis, represents climactic confrontation of the campaign

faction_indicators: Character class selection signals player identity: Fighter represents martial strength, Cleric represents faith/healing, Mage represents arcane knowledge
Multiplayer class choice in deathmatch contexts (up to 8 players supported) creates implicit team composition and role identity
danger_signals: Hub revisitation requirement creates potential for resource scarcity: players must manage ammo/mana across interconnected areas
Class fragility variance (Mage vulnerability) increases difficulty perception for skill-based communities
environmental_storytelling: Progression through elemental dungeons, wilderness, seminary, castle, and necropolis creates visual thematic escalation
Hub architecture itself communicates interconnectedness of the world: central areas serving as safe zones where players plan traversal strategy
Class-specific item behavior suggests tailored design for different player archetypes, reinforcing world accommodation of diverse heroes
